title: Balloon-Powered Car
materials:
Balloon
Plastic straw
4 bottle caps (wheels)
Skewers or toothpicks (axles)
Cardboard or lightweight plastic sheet
Tape
Scissors
instructions:
1. Cut the cardboard to a rectangle (around 15 x 10 cm) to form the car base.
2. Push skewers through the cardboard horizontally to make axles.
3. Attach bottle caps to the ends of skewers as wheels. Secure with tape if needed.
4. Tape a straw along the top of the cardboard so that the balloon's opening points backward.
5. Inflate the balloon, pinch it closed, and place it in the straw.
6. Release the balloon and watch your car move forward.
7. Try different balloon sizes or car weights to see which moves fastest.
what_you_learn: Air pushes the car forward when it escapes the balloon (Newton's Third Law). The experiment demonstrates action-reaction forces and friction effects.

title: Rubber Band Launcher
materials:
Rubber bands (various sizes)
Popsicle sticks (2–3)
Tape
Small lightweight object (paper ball, pom-pom)
instructions:
1. Stack two popsicle sticks together and secure with tape to make a launcher base.
2. Stretch a rubber band between the sticks so it can hold tension.
3. Place your object on the rubber band.
4. Pull back the rubber band to stretch it, and release to launch the object.
5. Measure how far the object travels.
6. Experiment with different rubber band thicknesses, lengths, and pull distances.
what_you_learn: Elastic potential energy stored in the rubber band converts to kinetic energy. You can observe how energy transfer and tension affect motion.

title: Simple Pendulum Experiment
materials:
String (~50 cm)
Small weight (washer, nut, toy)
Stopwatch
Tape or hook
instructions:
1. Tie the weight to one end of the string.
2. Attach the other end of the string to a hook or tape it to a sturdy table edge.
3. Pull the pendulum to the side so the string is at a slight angle.
4. Release the pendulum and start timing with a stopwatch.
5. Count 10 full swings back and forth. Record the time.
6. Repeat using strings of different lengths and compare results.
what_you_learn: Longer pendulum strings take more time to complete one swing (period). The experiment demonstrates how gravity and length affect oscillatory motion.

title: Water Bottle Rocket
materials:
2-liter plastic bottle
Water (~1/4 of bottle)
Cork
Bicycle pump with needle adapter
Safe outdoor space
instructions:
1. Fill the bottle 1/4 full with water.
2. Insert the cork into the bottle opening tightly.
3. Insert the bicycle pump needle through the cork to pressurize the bottle.
4. Place the bottle upside down on a flat, open surface outdoors.
5. Pump air slowly into the bottle until the cork pops and the bottle launches.
6. Measure flight distance or height. Experiment with different water levels.
what_you_learn: Pressurized air pushes water downward, propelling the bottle upward (Newton's Third Law). Changing water levels alters mass and thrust, demonstrating propulsion principles.

title: Homemade Electromagnet
materials:
Insulated copper wire (~2 m)
Iron nail (~5 cm)
AA battery
Paper clips
instructions:
1. Wrap the copper wire tightly around the nail, leaving ~5 cm free at each end.
2. Connect the free ends to the positive and negative terminals of the AA battery.
3. Hold the nail near paper clips and observe how it attracts them.
4. Experiment by changing the number of wire coils or using a larger battery.
5. Always disconnect the battery when not testing to avoid overheating the wire.
what_you_learn: Electric current through wire creates a magnetic field around the nail. This experiment demonstrates electromagnetism and how coil turns and current strength affect magnetic power.

title: Rube Goldberg Machine
materials:
Marbles or small balls
Cardboard ramps
Dominoes
Tape
Cups, toy cars, string, household objects
instructions:
1. Design a sequence of actions where one triggers the next, e.g., marble hits dominoes, which tip cups.
2. Set up ramps and objects according to your design.
3. Test each step individually to ensure it works.
4. Connect all steps, then trigger the first action.
5. Observe the chain reaction and adjust angles or spacing as needed.
what_you_learn: Demonstrates mechanical energy transfer and cause-and-effect relationships. Shows problem-solving, creativity, and design thinking principles.

title: Plant Observation Jar
materials:
Clear jar or container
Soil
Seeds (bean, sunflower)
Water
Paper and pen
instructions:
1. Fill the jar with soil to about 3/4 full.
2. Plant the seeds 1–2 cm deep in the soil.
3. Water lightly to moisten the soil.
4. Cover the jar loosely to retain moisture.
5. Place the jar in sunlight and observe daily.
6. Record changes in stem, leaf, and root growth.
what_you_learn: Plants grow toward light (phototropism). Seed germination and root development can be observed in real time.

title: Leaf Rubbings
materials:
Leaves of various shapes and sizes
Paper
Crayons or colored pencils
instructions:
1. Place a leaf with the vein side up under a sheet of paper.
2. Rub the crayon over the paper where the leaf is positioned.
3. Continue rubbing until the leaf pattern is clearly visible.
4. Compare patterns between different leaf types.
5. Experiment with layering multiple leaves to create designs.
what_you_learn: Leaf structure varies across species. Observing vein patterns teaches about nutrient transport in plants.

title: DIY Microscope Slides
materials:
Microscope
Glass slides and cover slips
Onion or leaf tissue
Dropper
Water
Tweezers
instructions:
1. Cut a thin piece of onion or leaf tissue using tweezers.
2. Place tissue on a glass slide.
3. Add one drop of water on top using the dropper.
4. Carefully cover with a cover slip, avoiding air bubbles.
5. Place under the microscope and adjust focus.
6. Observe cell shapes, nuclei, and other structures.
what_you_learn: Microscopes allow us to see cells invisible to the naked eye. Observing tissue reveals basic plant cell structure.

title: Worm Observation in Soil
materials:
Transparent container
Soil
Earthworms
Magnifying glass
Notebook
instructions:
1. Fill the container with a 5–10 cm layer of soil.
2. Gently place worms on the soil.
3. Observe movement and burrowing behaviors.
4. Use a magnifying glass to watch tunnels form.
5. Record observations in your notebook over several days.
what_you_learn: Worms improve soil aeration and nutrient distribution. Observation teaches about animal behavior and ecosystem roles.

title: Yeast Metabolism Experiment
materials:
Dry yeast
Sugar
Warm water
Balloon
Small bottle
Spoon
instructions:
1. Add 1 tsp sugar and 1 tsp yeast to the bottle.
2. Add 50 ml warm water and stir gently.
3. Stretch the balloon over the bottle top.
4. Place bottle in a warm spot.
5. Observe the balloon inflate as yeast produces carbon dioxide.
6. Experiment with different sugar amounts to see effect on inflation rate.
what_you_learn: Yeast produces gas through fermentation. Demonstrates how microorganisms metabolize sugar.

title: Extracting DNA from Strawberries
materials:
Strawberries (2–3)
Dish soap (1 tsp)
Salt (1/2 tsp)
Water (~50 ml)
Cold rubbing alcohol
Ziplock bag, coffee filter, small cup
instructions:
1. Place strawberries in bag and mash thoroughly.
2. Mix water, dish soap, and salt to create extraction solution.
3. Pour solution into the bag and mix gently.
4. Filter through a coffee filter into a cup.
5. Slowly pour cold alcohol along the side of the cup to form a separate layer.
6. Observe white, stringy DNA forming and spool it with a toothpick.
what_you_learn: DNA can be extracted from cells with simple chemicals. This shows basic genetics and molecular biology principles.

title: Baking Soda & Vinegar Volcano
materials:
Small container or bottle
Baking soda (~2 tbsp)
Vinegar (~50 ml)
Red food coloring (optional)
Tray or plate to contain mess
instructions:
1. Place the container on the tray to catch overflow.
2. Add 2 tablespoons of baking soda into the container.
3. Add a few drops of red food coloring for lava effect.
4. Slowly pour vinegar into the container.
5. Watch the foamy eruption as the liquid spills out.
6. Try adding more baking soda or vinegar to see how eruption size changes.
what_you_learn: Baking soda reacts with vinegar to produce carbon dioxide gas, creating pressure and fizz. Demonstrates an acid-base chemical reaction and gas production.

title: Oil & Water Density Experiment
materials:
Water (~1 cup)
Vegetable oil (~1 cup)
Food coloring (optional)
Tall clear glass
instructions:
1. Pour water into the glass.
2. Add a few drops of food coloring to make water visible.
3. Slowly pour oil on top of the water.
4. Observe that oil floats on top and does not mix with water.
5. Shake the glass gently to mix temporarily, then watch separation occur.
6. Try adding small objects like coins to see which layer they sink into.
what_you_learn: Oil is less dense than water, so it floats on top. This experiment demonstrates density and immiscible liquids.

title: Homemade pH Indicator
materials:
Red cabbage
Blender or grater
Boiling water
Strainer or filter
Lemon juice, vinegar, baking soda
instructions:
1. Chop the cabbage into small pieces.
2. Pour boiling water over the cabbage and let it sit for 10 minutes to extract pigment.
3. Strain the liquid to remove solid pieces.
4. Pour cabbage juice into small cups.
5. Add lemon juice, vinegar, or baking soda to each cup.
6. Observe color changes: acidic solutions turn red, neutral stay purple, basic solutions turn green/blue.
what_you_learn: The pigment in cabbage acts as a pH indicator. Demonstrates acid-base reactions and chemical changes.

title: Milk Surface Tension Experiment
materials:
Whole milk (~1 cup)
Food coloring
Dish soap (few drops)
Shallow plate
instructions:
1. Pour milk into the shallow plate until surface is covered.
2. Add 2–3 drops of different colored food coloring in the center.
3. Dip a toothpick into dish soap and touch it to the milk surface near the drops.
4. Watch the colors swirl and move rapidly across the milk.
5. Repeat touching different spots with the soapy toothpick.
6. Try with skim or 2% milk and observe differences in effect.
what_you_learn: Soap reduces surface tension, causing colored milk to move. Demonstrates molecular interactions and surface tension.

title: Crystal Growth Experiment
materials:
Borax or Epsom salt (~100 g)
Boiling water (~250 ml)
Jar
Pipe cleaners or string
Spoon
instructions:
1. Shape pipe cleaners into desired designs.
2. Boil water and dissolve borax or Epsom salt until saturated.
3. Suspend the pipe cleaner in solution using string so it does not touch the jar sides.
4. Leave the jar undisturbed overnight.
5. Observe crystals forming on the pipe cleaner.
6. Compare growth by changing solution concentration or temperature.
what_you_learn: Crystals form as the solution becomes supersaturated. Demonstrates solubility, nucleation, and crystal growth.

title: Electrolysis of Water
materials:
Glass jar (~250 ml)
Water (~200 ml)
Salt (pinch)
9V battery
Two pencils, sharpened at both ends
Wires with alligator clips
instructions:
1. Fill the jar with water and dissolve a pinch of salt.
2. Attach alligator clips to battery terminals and pencils.
3. Submerge pencils in water without touching each other.
4. Observe bubbles forming on each pencil.
5. Identify hydrogen (smaller bubbles) and oxygen (larger bubbles) at electrodes.
6. Try reversing battery connections to see gas production switch sides.
what_you_learn: Electricity can split water into hydrogen and oxygen gas. Demonstrates electrolysis and chemical decomposition reactions.

title: Popsicle Stick Bridge
materials:
Popsicle sticks (~50)
Glue
Small weights or coins
instructions:
1. Design your bridge by sketching a simple structure.
2. Glue popsicle sticks together to form base, beams, and supports.
3. Allow glue to dry completely before testing.
4. Gradually place weights on the bridge until it collapses.
5. Observe which parts broke first.
6. Redesign and reinforce weak areas to improve strength.
what_you_learn: Triangles and proper support increase bridge strength. Demonstrates structural engineering principles and load distribution.

title: Balloon Rocket
materials:
Balloon
String (~3 m)
Straw
Tape
instructions:
1. Thread string through straw and secure ends to two fixed points (chairs or door handles).
2. Inflate balloon without tying it, and tape it to the straw.
3. Hold the balloon closed and let go to launch along the string.
4. Observe the motion and speed.
5. Experiment with balloon sizes and angles of the string.
6. Try adding a small weight to see how distance changes.
what_you_learn: Escaping air propels the balloon along the string. Demonstrates Newton's Third Law and thrust concepts.

title: Rubber Band-Powered Helicopter
materials:
Paper
Scissors
Rubber bands
Paper clips
instructions:
1. Cut a paper helicopter shape with two rotors and a body.
2. Attach a paper clip at the bottom to weigh it slightly.
3. Loop a rubber band around the rotors for propulsion.
4. Twist the rubber band and release to make it spin and fly.
5. Observe rotor rotation and flight distance.
6. Experiment with rotor size, paper weight, or rubber band tension.
what_you_learn: Elastic energy turns into rotational kinetic energy. Demonstrates lift, drag, and rotational motion principles.

title: Catapult Building
materials:
Popsicle sticks (~10)
Rubber bands (~5)
Plastic spoon
Small objects (pom-poms, marshmallows)
instructions:
1. Stack 5 popsicle sticks and secure them with rubber bands for base.
2. Attach spoon to another stick and connect with rubber band for tension.
3. Place an object in the spoon and pull back to create tension.
4. Release to launch the object.
5. Adjust rubber band placement and angle for better distance.
6. Try different weights to test performance.
what_you_learn: Catapults store elastic potential energy that converts to kinetic energy. Demonstrates mechanical energy, tension, and projectile motion.

title: DIY Water Filtration System
materials:
Plastic bottle, cut in half
Gravel, sand, activated charcoal
Coffee filter
Dirty water
Spoon
instructions:
1. Invert top half of bottle to create a funnel.
2. Place a coffee filter at the bottom.
3. Layer activated charcoal, sand, and gravel inside the funnel.
4. Slowly pour dirty water through layers.
5. Observe particles being trapped in each layer.
6. Compare filtered water clarity with different layer arrangements.
what_you_learn: Each layer removes different contaminants. Demonstrates filtration, particle separation, and water purification principles.

title: Paper Circuit Project
materials:
Copper tape
LED lights
Coin battery
Paper
Tape or glue
instructions:
1. Draw a simple circuit path on paper using copper tape.
2. Place LEDs along the path with correct orientation.
3. Attach coin battery so the tape completes the circuit.
4. Ensure all connections are secure.
5. LEDs should light up; troubleshoot any loose connections.
6. Experiment with multiple LEDs, switches, or branching circuits.
what_you_learn: Electric current flows through a closed circuit to power LEDs. Demonstrates basic electricity, conductivity, and circuit design.

title: Static Electricity Water Bending
materials:
Inflated balloon
Wool sweater or clean, dry hair
Water tap or narrow stream of water
Sink or bowl
instructions:
1. Inflate a balloon and tie it closed.
2. Rub the balloon on a wool sweater or your hair for about 20 seconds to build up charge.
3. Turn on the tap so a thin, steady stream of water flows into the sink.
4. Slowly bring the charged side of the balloon close to the stream without touching it.
5. Watch the stream of water bend toward the balloon.
6. Recharge the balloon and try holding it at different distances to see how the bend changes.
what_you_learn: Rubbing the balloon gives it a static electric charge that attracts the slightly polar water molecules. This experiment demonstrates static electricity and how charged objects can exert force without touching.

title: Bending Pencil Light Refraction
materials:
Clear glass
Water (~250 ml)
Pencil or straight straw
instructions:
1. Fill the clear glass about three-quarters full with water.
2. Place a pencil into the glass so part of it stays above the water.
3. Look at the pencil from the side, level with the water surface.
4. Notice how the pencil appears bent or broken where it enters the water.
5. Slowly move your head up and down and watch the apparent bend change.
6. Try again with the glass empty and compare what you see.
what_you_learn: Light travels at different speeds in air and water, so it bends as it crosses the surface. This experiment demonstrates refraction and why objects can look shifted or broken when viewed through water.

title: Homemade Floating Compass
materials:
Sewing needle
Bar magnet or refrigerator magnet
Small piece of cork or foam
Shallow bowl of water
Actual compass or phone compass (for checking)
instructions:
1. Stroke the needle in one direction along one end of the magnet about 30 times, lifting the magnet away between each stroke.
2. Cut a thin slice of cork or a small piece of foam about 1 cm wide.
3. Lay the magnetized needle flat on top of the cork.
4. Float the cork gently in the center of a bowl of water.
5. Let it settle and watch the needle slowly turn to point north-south.
6. Compare the direction with a real compass, then spin the cork and confirm it returns to the same alignment.
what_you_learn: Stroking the needle aligns its atoms so it becomes a weak magnet that lines up with Earth's magnetic field. This experiment demonstrates magnetism and how a compass uses Earth's field to find direction.

title: Marble Roller Coaster Energy
materials:
Foam pipe insulation cut lengthwise, or flexible cardboard track (~2 m)
Marble
Tape
Books or chairs for height
Measuring tape
instructions:
1. Tape one end of the track high on a stack of books or a chair to make a starting hill.
2. Curve the rest of the track down and along the floor, adding a loop or a small second hill if you like.
3. Release a marble from the top without pushing it and watch it roll through the track.
4. Measure how far the marble travels past the end of the track.
5. Raise the starting height with more books and release the marble again.
6. Record the distance for each starting height and compare the results.
what_you_learn: The marble stores gravitational potential energy at the top that converts to kinetic energy as it rolls down. This experiment demonstrates energy transfer and how starting height affects speed and distance.

title: Sound Wave Salt Patterns
materials:
Large empty can or plastic tub with both ends open, or a bowl
Plastic wrap or a cut balloon
Rubber band
Salt (~1 tsp)
Speaker or phone playing tones
instructions:
1. Stretch plastic wrap or a piece of cut balloon tightly over the open top of the can and hold it with a rubber band, making a flat drum-like surface.
2. Sprinkle about a teaspoon of salt evenly across the stretched surface.
3. Place a speaker or phone close to the surface, facing it, playing a steady low tone.
4. Slowly raise the pitch of the tone and watch the salt jump and gather into patterns.
5. Note which pitches make the salt form clear shapes or lines.
6. Adjust how tightly the surface is stretched and repeat to see how the patterns change.
what_you_learn: Sound waves make the stretched surface vibrate, and the salt collects at the still points between vibrations. This experiment demonstrates standing waves and how frequency shapes vibration patterns.

title: Air Pressure Can Crusher
materials:
Empty aluminum soda can
Water (~1 tbsp)
Tongs
Stove or hot plate
Large bowl of ice water
Oven mitt
instructions:
1. Ask an adult to help, as this experiment uses heat and steam that can burn. An adult should supervise every step.
2. Put about one tablespoon of water into the empty can.
3. With an adult, heat the can on the stove until steam flows steadily from the opening for about 30 seconds.
4. Prepare a large bowl of ice water next to the stove.
5. Using tongs, quickly lift the can and flip it upside down into the ice water so the opening goes under the surface.
6. Watch the can crush itself almost instantly, then discuss what happened.
what_you_learn: Heating turns the water to steam that pushes out air, and cooling condenses the steam so outside air pressure crushes the can. This experiment demonstrates air pressure and how gases expand and contract with temperature.

title: Celery Transpiration Colors
materials:
Celery stalks with leaves (2-3)
Clear cups
Water
Food coloring (red or blue)
Knife (adult use)
instructions:
1. Fill each clear cup about halfway with water.
2. Add 10-15 drops of food coloring to each cup and stir.
3. Ask an adult to trim about 1 cm off the bottom of each celery stalk.
4. Stand one celery stalk in each cup of colored water.
5. Leave the cups in a bright spot and check every few hours for a full day.
6. After a day, look at the leaves and ask an adult to slice a stalk crosswise to see the colored tubes inside.
what_you_learn: Water moves up the celery through tiny tubes and the dye reveals the path all the way to the leaves. This experiment demonstrates transpiration and how plants transport water from roots to leaves.

title: Fingerprint Patterns Study
materials:
Pencil
White paper
Clear tape
Magnifying glass
Several volunteers
instructions:
1. Rub a pencil firmly on a small patch of paper to make a dark graphite square.
2. Press one fingertip onto the graphite so it picks up the powder.
3. Stick a piece of clear tape over your fingertip, then peel it off to lift the print.
4. Place the tape on white paper to reveal the fingerprint.
5. Use a magnifying glass to look for loops, whorls, and arches in the pattern.
6. Collect prints from friends and family, then sort them by pattern type and compare.
what_you_learn: Everyone has unique fingerprint ridges that fall into a few basic pattern types. This experiment demonstrates individual variation and how traits can be both inherited and unique.

title: Bird Feeder Observation Journal
materials:
Empty plastic bottle or cardboard tube
Bird seed
String
Scissors
Notebook and pen
Optional: field guide or bird ID app
instructions:
1. With adult help, cut two small holes near the bottom of a plastic bottle and push a stick or spoon through to make perches.
2. Cut small feeding openings above the perches and fill the bottle with bird seed.
3. Tie string around the neck and hang the feeder from a tree branch or hook outdoors.
4. Watch quietly from a window at the same time each day for one week.
5. In your notebook, record the date, time, weather, and each bird species you see.
6. At the end of the week, count how many different species visited and note which foods and times were most popular.
what_you_learn: Different birds visit at different times and prefer different foods and perches. This experiment demonstrates observation skills, biodiversity, and how animals adapt their feeding behavior.

title: Seed Dispersal Models
materials:
Paper
Scissors
Paper clips
Tape
Cotton or tissue
Velcro or a sock, dried burrs
Small dried beans or beads
instructions:
1. Build a helicopter seed by cutting a paper strip, splitting the top into two wings, and adding a paper clip at the bottom.
2. Build a fluffy seed by taping a light bead to a tuft of cotton so it can float on air.
3. Build a hitchhiker seed by attaching a small hook or burr to a bead so it can cling to fabric.
4. Drop the helicopter and fluffy seeds from the same height and watch how they travel.
5. Press the hitchhiker seed against a sock and check whether it sticks.
6. Record how far each model moves and which method spreads seeds farthest.
what_you_learn: Plants spread their seeds using wind, animals, and other clever shapes to travel far from the parent. This experiment demonstrates seed dispersal strategies and how structure helps plants survive and reproduce.

title: Bread Mold Growth Study
materials:
3 slices of bread
3 sealable plastic bags
Water spray or dropper
Labels and marker
Notebook
instructions:
1. Label three bags: dry, damp, and damp-dark.
2. Place one dry bread slice in the first bag and seal it.
3. Lightly spray or sprinkle a few drops of water on two other slices, then seal each in its own bag.
4. Keep the dry bag and one damp bag on a warm counter, and put the second damp bag in a dark cupboard.
5. Do not open the bags again, as mold spores should not be breathed in; only observe through the sealed plastic.
6. Check each bag daily for one to two weeks, recording when mold appears and how much grows in each condition, then throw the sealed bags away without opening them.
what_you_learn: Mold grows fastest where there is moisture and warmth, showing what microorganisms need to thrive. This experiment demonstrates decomposition and how environmental conditions control microbial growth.

title: Balloon Lung Model
materials:
Clear plastic bottle, cut in half at the bottom (adult use)
2 small balloons
1 large balloon
Plastic straw or Y-shaped tubing
Rubber band
Tape or modeling clay
instructions:
1. Ask an adult to cut off the bottom of a plastic bottle.
2. Attach a small balloon to the end of a straw and lower it inside the bottle so the balloon hangs down and the straw sticks out the top; seal around the straw with clay.
3. This small balloon inside represents a lung.
4. Cut the neck off the large balloon and stretch the wide part tightly over the open bottom of the bottle, securing it with a rubber band. This is the diaphragm.
5. Gently pull the stretched bottom balloon downward and watch the lung balloon inside inflate.
6. Push the bottom balloon back up and watch the lung balloon deflate, then repeat to model breathing.
what_you_learn: Pulling the diaphragm down lowers the pressure inside so air rushes in to fill the lung. This experiment demonstrates how breathing works and how air pressure drives inhalation and exhalation.

title: Penny Cleaning Acid Test
materials:
Several dull, dirty pennies
White vinegar (~50 ml)
Salt (1 tsp)
Small bowl
Water
Paper towel
instructions:
1. Pour about 50 ml of vinegar into a small bowl and stir in one teaspoon of salt until it dissolves.
2. Set aside one dirty penny so you can compare it later.
3. Dip another penny halfway into the vinegar-salt mixture and hold it for about 20 seconds.
4. Lift it out and compare the dipped half with the untouched half.
5. Drop several more pennies fully into the mixture and wait 5 minutes.
6. Rinse the pennies with water, dry them on a paper towel, and compare their shine to the untreated penny.
what_you_learn: The acid in vinegar combined with salt dissolves the dull copper oxide coating and reveals shiny metal. This experiment demonstrates acid-base chemistry and how acids react with metal oxides.

title: Homemade Polymer Slime
materials:
White school glue (~120 ml)
Water (~120 ml)
Baking soda (1/2 tsp)
Contact lens solution containing boric acid (~1 tbsp)
Food coloring (optional)
Bowl and spoon
instructions:
1. Pour about 120 ml of white glue into a bowl.
2. Stir in about 120 ml of water and a few drops of food coloring if you want.
3. Mix in half a teaspoon of baking soda until fully combined.
4. Add contact lens solution one tablespoon at a time, stirring after each addition.
5. Keep stirring until the mixture pulls away from the bowl and becomes stretchy.
6. Knead the slime with your hands for a minute, adding a tiny bit more solution if it is too sticky, then wash your hands when finished.
what_you_learn: The contact lens solution links the glue molecules into long chains that trap the liquid, forming a stretchy polymer. This experiment demonstrates polymers and how cross-linking changes a material from liquid to gel.

title: Lemon Juice Invisible Ink
materials:
Lemon juice (~2 tbsp)
Water (few drops)
Cotton swab or thin paintbrush
White paper
Small bowl
Desk lamp with an incandescent bulb or an iron (adult use)
instructions:
1. Mix about two tablespoons of lemon juice with a few drops of water in a small bowl.
2. Dip a cotton swab into the mixture and write a secret message on white paper.
3. Let the paper dry completely so the writing becomes invisible.
4. Ask an adult to help with the heat step, as hot lamps and irons can burn.
5. With an adult, hold the paper near a warm incandescent bulb or press it with a warm iron.
6. Watch the hidden message turn brown and appear as the paper heats up.
what_you_learn: Lemon juice weakens the paper and browns at a lower temperature than plain paper when heated. This experiment demonstrates oxidation and how heat can trigger a chemical color change.

title: Balloon Gas Race: Pop Rocks vs Baking Soda
materials:
2 small bottles
2 balloons
Pop Rocks candy (1 pack)
Baking soda (1 tsp)
Vinegar (~50 ml)
Soda or carbonated water (~50 ml)
Funnel or spoon
instructions:
1. Pour about 50 ml of vinegar into the first bottle.
2. Use a funnel to add one teaspoon of baking soda into the first balloon.
3. Pour about 50 ml of carbonated soda into the second bottle and add a pack of Pop Rocks into the second balloon.
4. Stretch each balloon over its bottle without letting the contents fall in yet.
5. Lift both balloons at the same time so the powder and candy drop into the liquids together.
6. Watch both balloons inflate and record which one fills faster and larger.
what_you_learn: The baking soda and vinegar reaction produces carbon dioxide quickly, while Pop Rocks release trapped carbon dioxide more slowly. This experiment demonstrates gas production and lets you compare the rate of two different reactions.

title: Rock Candy Sugar Crystals
materials:
Sugar (~500 g)
Water (~250 ml)
Wooden skewer or string
Clothespin
Tall glass or jar
Saucepan (adult use)
Food coloring (optional)
instructions:
1. Ask an adult to help, as this uses boiling water that can scald. An adult should handle the heat throughout.
2. With an adult, heat 250 ml of water in a saucepan and stir in sugar a little at a time until no more will dissolve, about 500 g.
3. Add a few drops of food coloring if desired, then let the syrup cool for 15 minutes.
4. Dampen a skewer, roll it in a little dry sugar, and let it dry so crystals have a surface to grow on.
5. Pour the cooled syrup into a glass and suspend the skewer with a clothespin so it does not touch the sides or bottom.
6. Leave the glass undisturbed for about one week, checking daily as sugar crystals slowly grow on the skewer.
what_you_learn: As the water slowly evaporates, dissolved sugar rejoins into solid crystals on the skewer. This experiment demonstrates solubility, supersaturation, and how crystals form from a solution over time.

title: Kid-Safe Elephant Toothpaste
materials:
Empty plastic bottle
3% hydrogen peroxide (~100 ml)
Dish soap (1 tbsp)
Warm water (~1 tbsp)
Active dry yeast (1 tbsp)
Food coloring
Tray, safety goggles, gloves
instructions:
1. Ask an adult to supervise, and have everyone wear safety goggles, since hydrogen peroxide can irritate eyes and skin.
2. Stand the bottle in the center of a tray to catch the foam.
3. Pour about 100 ml of 3% hydrogen peroxide into the bottle, then add a squirt of dish soap and a few drops of food coloring.
4. In a separate cup, stir one tablespoon of yeast into one tablespoon of warm water and wait one minute.
5. Quickly pour the yeast mixture into the bottle and step back.
6. Watch the foamy column push out of the bottle, and once it cools, wipe it up with water since it is just soapy water and yeast.
what_you_learn: The yeast speeds up the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide into water and oxygen, and the soap traps the oxygen as foam. This experiment demonstrates a catalyzed decomposition reaction and how a catalyst speeds up chemical change.

title: Spaghetti and Marshmallow Tower
materials:
Uncooked spaghetti (~30 strands)
Mini marshmallows (~30)
Ruler
Flat table
instructions:
1. Push marshmallows onto the ends of spaghetti strands to connect them.
2. Build small triangles first, since triangles are strong shapes.
3. Join the triangles into a base, then build upward.
4. Keep the structure balanced so it does not lean or tip.
5. Measure the height of your tower with a ruler once it stands on its own.
6. Rebuild a second design and try to make it taller and more stable than the first.
what_you_learn: Triangles distribute weight and make structures stable and strong. This experiment demonstrates structural engineering and how shape affects the stability of a building.

title: Paper Airplane Design Testing
materials:
Several sheets of paper
Measuring tape
Open hallway or room
Notebook and pen
Paper clips (optional)
instructions:
1. Fold three different paper airplane designs, such as a dart, a glider, and a wide-wing plane.
2. Mark a starting line on the floor to throw from.
3. Throw each plane three times using about the same force, and measure how far each flight goes.
4. Record the distances for every throw in your notebook.
5. Add a paper clip to the nose of one plane and test whether it flies farther or straighter.
6. Compare your results and decide which design flew best and why.
what_you_learn: Wing shape and weight balance change how far and straight a plane flies. This experiment demonstrates aerodynamics, lift, and the engineering practice of testing and improving designs.

title: Egg Drop Lander
materials:
Raw egg
Cotton balls, sponges, or bubble wrap
Small box or plastic cup
Tape
Straws or craft sticks
Plastic bag
Measuring tape
instructions:
1. Design a container that will protect a raw egg from a fall using cushioning and supports.
2. Wrap the egg in cotton balls, sponge, or bubble wrap and place it inside a box or cup.
3. Add straws or craft sticks as a frame to absorb impact, and tape everything securely.
4. Optionally attach a plastic bag as a parachute to slow the fall.
5. Drop the lander from about 2 meters over a safe outdoor surface.
6. Open it and check whether the egg cracked, then adjust the design and test again from a greater height.
what_you_learn: Cushioning and structure spread out the impact force and slow the egg's stop. This experiment demonstrates force, impulse, and how engineers design to protect fragile cargo.

title: Straw Roller Coaster
materials:
Paper straws or bendy straws (~20)
Tape
Cardboard base
Small marble or bead
Scissors
instructions:
1. Tape a starting support to the cardboard base to hold the top of your track high.
2. Cut straws lengthwise and tape them end to end to form a channel for the marble.
3. Build a downhill slope from the starting height, then add gentle curves.
4. Try adding a small hill or a curved turn, keeping each rise lower than the start.
5. Roll a marble from the top and watch it travel the track.
6. Adjust the slopes and supports until the marble completes the whole track smoothly.
what_you_learn: The marble needs enough starting height to keep enough energy for every hill and curve. This experiment demonstrates energy conservation and the engineering trade-offs in designing a working track.

title: Simple Pulley Lifting System
materials:
String (~2 m)
2 empty thread spools or small pulleys
Broomstick or dowel between two chairs
Small bucket or cup
Weights (coins or small toys)
Tape
instructions:
1. Rest a broomstick across the backs of two chairs to make a sturdy overhead bar.
2. For a single fixed pulley, hang a spool on the bar and run string over it, tying a small bucket to one end.
3. Add weights to the bucket and lift it by pulling the other end of the string, noticing the effort needed.
4. Now set up a second spool attached to the bucket and thread the string down, around it, and back up to build a movable pulley system.
5. Lift the same weight again and compare how hard you have to pull.
6. Record how the pulling effort and the length of string pulled change between the two setups.
what_you_learn: A movable pulley lets you lift a load with less force but requires pulling more string. This experiment demonstrates mechanical advantage and how simple machines trade force for distance.

title: Cardboard Automaton with Cams
materials:
Cardboard box
Wooden skewers or dowels
Hot glue or strong tape (adult use for hot glue)
Cardboard or bottle caps for cams
Straws
Scissors
Small paper figure
instructions:
1. Cut holes in the top and sides of the box so a skewer can pass through as a turning handle axle.
2. Push straws through the side holes to act as smooth bearings, then slide a skewer through as the crank axle.
3. Cut a round or egg-shaped cam from cardboard or use a bottle cap and attach it to the axle inside the box.
4. Stand a vertical skewer through the top hole so its bottom rests on the cam, with a paper figure taped on top.
5. Ask an adult to help with any hot glue, as it can burn.
6. Turn the crank and watch the cam push the vertical rod up and down, making the figure bob, then try different cam shapes to change the motion.
what_you_learn: The rotating cam converts spinning motion into up-and-down motion of the follower rod. This experiment demonstrates mechanisms and how cams transform one type of motion into another.
